coredata.dc:title: Hydrodynamic cavitation synthesis of water-soluble CdSe quantum dots: Effect of venturi tube parameters on size distribution and fluorescence properties, with application in Fe3+ detection

Wang, Jun
Fang, Dawei
coredata.dc:description:
Background Fe3+ is an important trace element in life metabolism and also the main component of industrial pollutant. How to improve the sensitivity of Fe3+ detection is of great significance in biological and environmental analyses. Fluorescence probe for Fe3+ detection has high sensitivity, but it is difficult to prepare a probe with high sensitivity. CdSe quantum dots (QDs) have the fast fluorescence response to Fe3+, which can be considered as an ideal fluorescent probe. In this study, CdSe QDs were prepared on large scale by the hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) technology using the physicochemical effect generated by cavitation bubble rupture.
Results In venturi tube HC system, the sphalerite CdSe QDs (12 h) prepared by the control variable method have 1.83 nm average particle size, 1255 ns fluorescence lifetime and 87 nm Stokes shift. In addition, the prepared CdSe QDs (12 h) solution still has good stability after six weeks of storage. And then, the CdSe QDs (12 h) are used as fluorescence probe to detect Fe3+ and the limit of detection is 4.98 μmol/L, which is better than most existing fluorescence probes reported in references. Moreover, the concentrations of purchased FeCl3 solutions are measured by using the fluorescence probe method and the measured results are comparable with those obtained by using the inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer. This indicates that this method can be used to determine the concentration of Fe3+ in real samples, which has a high accuracy.
Significance In this work, CdSe QDs prepared by HC method is used as fluorescence probe to detect Fe3+. This research not only provides reference for the large-scale preparation of CdSe QDs, but also helps to understand CdSe QDs detection mechanism as fluorescence probe. The CdSe QDs as probe for Fe3+ analysis have the advantages of low limit of detection and high sensitivity. This has potential applications for Fe3+ accurate detection in life medicine and materials science.

head.ce:title: Hydrodynamic cavitation synthesis of water-soluble CdSe quantum dots: Effect of venturi tube parameters on size distribution and fluorescence properties, with application in Fe3+ detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fe3+ is an important trace element in life metabolism and also the main component of industrial pollutant. How to improve the sensitivity of Fe3+ detection is of great significance in biological and environmental analyses. Fluorescence probe for Fe3+ detection has high sensitivity, but it is difficult to prepare a probe with high sensitivity. CdSe quantum dots (QDs) have the fast fluorescence response to Fe3+, which can be considered as an ideal fluorescent probe. In this study, CdSe QDs were prepared on large scale by the hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) technology using the physicochemical effect generated by cavitation bubble rupture.

In venturi tube HC system, the sphalerite CdSe QDs (12 h) prepared by the control variable method have 1.83 nm average particle size, 1255 ns fluorescence lifetime and 87 nm Stokes shift. In addition, the prepared CdSe QDs (12 h) solution still has good stability after six weeks of storage. And then, the CdSe QDs (12 h) are used as fluorescence probe to detect Fe3+ and the limit of detection is 4.98 μmol/L, which is better than most existing fluorescence probes reported in references. Moreover, the concentrations of purchased FeCl3 solutions are measured by using the fluorescence probe method and the measured results are comparable with those obtained by using the inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer. This indicates that this method can be used to determine the concentration of Fe3+ in real samples, which has a high accuracy.

In this work, CdSe QDs prepared by HC method is used as fluorescence probe to detect Fe3+. This research not only provides reference for the large-scale preparation of CdSe QDs, but also helps to understand CdSe QDs detection mechanism as fluorescence probe. The CdSe QDs as probe for Fe3+ analysis have the advantages of low limit of detection and high sensitivity. This has potential applications for Fe3+ accurate detection in life medicine and materials science.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe) is one of the important trace elements in the metabolic process of life systems. Fe3+ can participate in oxygen metabolism and promote electron transfer during DNA and RNA synthesis [1]. However, some studies have shown that the deficiency and excess of Fe3+ both can cause a variety of diseases. For example, the deficiency of Fe3+ can lead to anemia, liver damage, diabetes, cancer and Parkinson's diseases [2] and the excess of Fe3+ can lead to oxidative stress, hemochromatosis, Alzheimer's and neurodegenerative diseases [3]. In addition, as a major component of industrial pollutants, Fe3+ is constantly discharged into the ocean, which has caused a serious environmental pollution problems [4]. Therefore, the selective detection of Fe3+ is of great significance in biological and environmental systems. Until now, numerous methods have been developed for the determination of Fe3+, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], electrochemistry [6], fluorescence method [7] and so on. Among them, the fluorescence method is the most appealing due to the advantages of high sensitivity, good reproducibility, small sample amount required and fast analysis speed [8]. Recently, numerous nanomaterials that can be used as fluorescent probes, such as quantum dots [9,10], metallic hydroxides [11] and metal-organic frameworks [12] have been reported to be used for the determination of Fe3+. It is noted that it is still a challenge to develop a fluorescent probe that not only has high sensitivity to Fe3+ but also is not affected by other metal ions. Therefore, the design and development of appropriate fluorescent probes, along with an in-depth understanding of the detection mechanism of Fe3+, are important in the applications of biological and environmental analysis.

Quantum dots (QDs) have excellent optical properties such as wide excitation spectrum, narrow emission spectrum and high photoluminescence intensity. Thus, QDs are considered as promising materials in biomedical diagnostics, light-emitting diodes, solar cells and sensors [13,14]. Cadmium selenide quantum dots (CdSe QDs) have been studied as a hot topic in the field of nanomaterials for a long time because of their high size uniformity, good crystal quality and high photoluminescence intensity [15]. Compared with other nanostructured CdSe such as nanosheets and nanotubes, CdSe QDs not only have the characteristics of small size, large specific surface area and tunable fluorescence emission wavelength, but also have good photoelectric properties and high stability [16]. Based on the above advantages, CdSe QDs are considered to have great application potential in the field of chemical analysis and detection [17]. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop a method for large-scale preparation of CdSe QDs to meet the needs of applications.

In general, QDs can be prepared via two routes, namely the top-down method and the bottom-up method. The bottom-up method includes thermal injection [18], hydrothermal [19] and microfluidic methods [20], in which small-molecular precursors are usually used to synthesize QDs. These methods usually require rapid adjustment of the reaction temperature to obtain QDs with good crystallinity and uniform particle size and shape. However, with the increase of fluid volume and container size, temperature control will become an issue during large-scale preparation, which brings difficulties to the preparation of QDs and subsequent purification steps. In summary, although the bottom-up method has the advantages of no additional steps and a wide range of temperature selection, it does not meet the large-scale demand of the current market, thus limiting the development and application of CdSe QDs. Contrary to the bottom-up method, the top-down method refers to obtain QDs by decomposing and breaking up large-sized materials, such as laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation and chemical vapor deposition. However, so far, there are few reports on the use of top-down method to prepare CdSe QDs.

Hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) is an emerging technology that can strengthen various physical and chemical processes. It has a wide range of applications in the fields of organic pollutants degradation [21], activated sludge treatment [22], microbial inactivation [23], mineral flotation [24] and so on. HC technology has a good prospect in industrial applications because of easy scale-up and low energy consumption [25]. The HC effect is usually caused by a pressure change of the fluid passing through the contraction section (such as venturi tube and orifice plate with different geometries). When the fluid passes through the contraction section, cavities are produced due to the change in fluid pressure [26]. With the decrease of pressure, these resulting cavities grow into cavitation bubbles by absorbing energy and subsequently collapse, releasing a large amount of energy, resulting in mechanical, chemical and thermal effects [27]. These effects provide the conditions for the preparation of nanomaterials [28]. In addition, it has been shown that nanomaterials with high stability can be prepared using the HC method. Zhang et al. demonstrated that the HC is an efficient method for the formation of O/W emulsion with an average droplet size of 27 nm and high stability. The formed O/W emulsion exhibited good physical stability during 8 months [29]. Carpenter et al. used the HC method to prepare mustard oil in O/W emulsions. Under the action of centrifugal force and thermal stress, the kinetic stability of mustard oil nano-emulsion can reach 3 months. In addition, the study also showed that the energy efficiency of HC treatment is 11 times that of ultrasonic treatment [30]. In conclusion, it is feasible to prepare high-quality CdSe QDs on a large scale due to the unique internal structure of the HC device and the easy control of conditions such as reaction temperature, pressure and fluid rate.

In this study, water-soluble CdSe QDs are prepared in a HC reactor on a large scale and used as a fluorescent probe for the selective detection of Fe3+. The effects of cycle time, inlet pressure and geometric parameters of venturi tubes on the crystal structure, particle size, optical property and stability of the prepared CdSe QDs are investigated. The possible mechanism of preparing CdSe QDs by using HC method is also given. In addition, the fluorescence response of CdSe QDs to 10 different metal cations is also studied. It is found that Fe3+ has a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of CdSe QDs. After that, the detection mechanism is further determined by studying the effect of Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of CdSe QDs.

Cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, AR), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, AR), Se power (AR) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR) were gained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Regent Factory in P. R. China. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, AR), sodium chloride (NaCl, AR) and stannous chloride (SnCl2, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co. Ltd. in P. R. China. Nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, AR) and magnesium chloride (MgCl2, AR) were bought from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. in P. R. China. Barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2·2H2O, AR), calcium chloride (CaCl2, AR), ferric chloride (FeCl3, AR) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2, AR) were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd. in P. R. China. Copper Chloride (CuCl2, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. in P. R. China. All reagents were used directly without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used in the whole research.

Firstly, 10.27 g (0.13 mol) Se powder and 16.39 g (0.13 mol) sodium sulfite were dissolved in 500 mL deionized water and stirred until fully dissolved to form selenosulfate (Na2SeSO3) solution. After that, 5.20 g (0.13 mol) NaOH was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water and then added to the above Na2SeSO3 solution to form solution A containing NaHSe. Secondly, 30.74 g (0.13 mol) Cd(NO3)2·4H2O was dissolved in 400 mL deionized water to form Cd(NO3)2 solution B. Thirdly, solution A and solution B were mixed and heated to 160 °C for 6.0 h with magnetic stirring. Finally, the sample was left standing and washed five times using deionized water to obtain CdSe precipitates.

The possible reaction processes are as follow:
Se + Na2SO3 → Na2SeSO3
NaOH + Na2SeSO3 → NaHSe + Na2SO4
NaHSe + Cd(NO3)2·4H2O → CdSe (precipitates) + NaNO3 + 4H2O

The freshly prepared CdSe precipitates were diluted to 5.0 L by using deionized water and placed in the holding tank of the HC reactor. At the beginning of this experiment, the valve, the power supply of the cooling water system and the self-priming pump were opened successively to make the solution enter the HC device. In this study, the effects of different HC operation parameters, namely cycle time (6.0 h, 9.0 h and 12 h), inlet pressure (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar), as well as venturi tube geometric parameters, that is throat diameter (1.0 mm, 2.0 mm and 4.0 mm), half divergent angle (4.0°, 6.0° and 8.0°) and half convergent angle (20°, 22° and 24°), on the particle sizes, morphologies, optical properties and stability of CdSe QDs were investigated. At the end of the cycling experiment, the resulting liquids were filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane to attain CdSe QDs solution.

The crystal structure and elemental composition of the CdSe precipitates and CdSe QDs samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements on an X-ray diffractometer (D-8, Bruker-AXS, Germany). The morphology and microstructure of samples were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Corporation, US). Elemental compositions of samples were observed by an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer instrument (EDX, S-4800, Hitachi Corporation, Japan). The surface chemical state and valence state distribution of samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher Corporation, China). The concentrations of CdSe QDs were determined by inductive coupled Plasma emission spectrometer (ICP, ICP-OES730, Agilent, USA). A UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-3310, Hitachi, Japan) with a Xe lamp as a light source and a grating monochromator as the excitation source was used to record the UV–vis absorption spectra of CdSe QDs solutions. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse 3000, Agilent, USA) was used to record the fluorescence spectra of CdSe QDs solutions. A transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS-1000, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK) was used to record the fluorescence lifetime of QDs. The concentrations of CdSe QDs were determined by inductive coupled Plasma emission spectrometer (ICP, ICP-OES730, Agilent, USA).

The effect of HC can be characterized by a cavitation number (C v), which can be calculated by using formula (1) or formula (2):
(1)
C v
=
P 2
−
P v
0.5 ρ
v 2
(2)
C v
=
P 2
−
P v
P 1
−
P 2
Where, P 1 is the upstream inlet pressure of the HC device, P 2 is the downstream recovery pressure of the HC device, P v is the saturated vapor pressure of the liquid, ρ is the density of the solution and v is the flow velocity at the contraction section. When cavitation phenomenon occurs, C v is less than 1.0. Generally, when C v is between 0.1 and 1.0, it can be considered that a strong HC phenomenon has occurred. And that, the smaller C v is, the stronger HC phenomenon is [31,32]. However, when C v is less than 0.1, the super-cavitation phenomenon occurs, which reduces the HC strength.

Aqueous solutions containing 10 μmol/L different metal ions (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Na+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Zn2+ and Mg2+) are prepared. 2.0 mL solutions containing above metal ions are added to 2.0 mL CdSe QDs (12 h) solution, respectively, and the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions are measured. In order to evaluate the sensitivity of Fe3+ detection and study the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ on CdSe QDs, Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations (0–20 μmol/L) are added to 2.0 mL CdSe QDs (12 h) solution, respectively, and the fluorescence spectra are measured after magnetic stirring for 5.0 min. All samples are excited under wavelength light of 272 nm. Finally, the quenching mechanism is further confirmed by using time-resolved photoluminescence tests of CdSe QDs (12 h) solution and 1.0 μmol/L Fe3+/CdSe QDs (12 h) solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of HC cycle time and inlet pressure on particle size, morphology, components, and optical properties of CdSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) technique is used to analyze the crystal structure and chemical composition of the prepared samples. The XRD images of CdSe precipitates and CdSe QDs prepared within different HC cycle time are shown in Fig. 1 (a) and the corresponding standard card of sphalerite CdSe (JCPDS #19–0191) is also given as a comparison of characteristic peaks. As can be seen in the figure, both CdSe precipitates and CdSe QDs have three diffraction peaks located at 2θ values of 25.35°, 42.01° and 49.70° [33], respectively. They correspond to three crystal planes of (111), (220) and (311), which indicate that the CdSe precipitates and CdSe QDs possess the sphalerite crystal structure. Furthermore, compared with CdSe precipitates, CdSe QDs have a wider diffraction peak and the diffraction peaks broaden with the extension of HC cycle time. The broadening of the diffraction peaks may be caused by the decrease in particle size [34].

The particle size of CdSe QDs can also be calculated according to Debye-Scherrer formula, as shown in formula (3) [35]:
(3)
D =
K λ
B
1 / 2
cos
θ

Thereinto, D is the average size of the crystallite. K is the Scherrer constant related to the particle shape, for spherical particles, K = 0.89. λ is the X-ray wavelength, for Cu kα target, λ = 0.15406 nm. B 1/2 is the full-width half-maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak and θ is the Bragg diffraction angle. After calculation, the average particle sizes of the CdSe QDs (6.0 h), CdSe (9.0 h) and CdSe (12 h) are 2.95 nm, 2.36 nm and 2.09 nm, respectively. It can be predicted that with the extension of HC time, smaller CdSe QDs particle can be obtained.

In particular, to further study the elemental composition and chemical state of the prepared sample, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CdSe QDs (9.0 h) are determined and the related results are shown in Fig. 1(b–d). The characteristic signals of Cd, Se and O elements can be intuitively observed, which certify the presence of these three elements in the prepared sample. The peaks located at binding energies of 405.0 eV, 411.7 eV, 617.0 eV, 651.0 eV and 1109.8 eV can be attributed to 3d5, 3d3, 3p3 and 3p1/2 characteristic signals and Auger peak of Cd2+ in CdSe QDs, respectively. The Se 3d peak with binding energy of 54.2 eV and Auger peaks with binding energies of 137.8 eV and 178.7 eV are attributed to of Se2− in CdSe QDs, respectively. The weak O 1s peak appears at binding energy of 531 eV, which may be because the oxygen molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the sample.

Subsequently, the XPS fine spectra are used to reveal the chemical valence states of Cd and Se elements and the relevant results are displayed in Fig. 1(c and d). In Fig. 1(c), the two peaks at the binding energies of 405.0 eV and 411.7 eV correspond to the Cd 3d3 and Cd 3d5 characteristic signals of Cd2+ in CdSe QDs [36], respectively. In Fig. 1(d), the two peaks at the binding energies of 54.2 eV and 54.9 eV correspond to the Se 3d3 and Se 3d5 characteristic signals of Se2− in CdSe QDs [37], respectively. Based on the above analyses, it can be inferred that CdSe QDs have been successfully prepared and the valence states of Cd and Se are +2 and −2, respectively.

The microscopic morphology of CdSe QDs is observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a–c) are TEM images of CdSe QDs obtained within HC cycle time of 6.0 h, 9.0 h and 12 h, respectively, and the insets in the upper right corner of three figures show the particle size distribution of the prepared CdSe QDs. It can be observed that the prepared CdSe QDs are spherical and have good dispersion. Moreover, the average particle sizes of CdSe QDs are 2.32 nm (6.0 h), 2.28 nm (9.0 h) and 1.93 nm (12 h) and the size distribution ranges of CdSe QDs are 1.72 nm–4.22 nm, 1.53 nm–3.52 nm and 1.18 nm–3.07 nm, respectively. Namely, with the extension of HC cycle time, the particle size of CdSe QDs decreases gradually and the size distribution narrows. This is because CdSe QDs are processed more times in the HC device with the extension of HC cycle time, which eventually leads to a gradual reduction of the CdSe QDs particle size. Obviously, the average particle sizes obtained in the TEM images are similar to those calculated by the Debye-Scherrer formula. Ulteriorly, the lattice fringes of the CdSe QDs (9.0 h) are analyzed in Fig. 2(b–1) and the lattice spacing is calculated to reveal the crystal morphology of the sample. It can be confirmed that the lattice spacing of 0.28 nm corresponds to the (311) crystal plane of sphalerite CdSe. This result is consistent with the XRD characterization result.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared CdSe QDs solutions are tested to study the optical performance and the results are shown in Fig. 2(d). The UV–vis spectra of three CdSe QDs solutions all display an absorption peak at 276 nm. Compared with the absorption peak (712 nm) of bulk CdSe [38], the UV–vis absorption peaks of three CdSe QDs solutions all show a significant blue shift. This indicates that small size CdSe QDs can be obtained using the HC method. Furthermore, with the extension of the HC cycle time, the absorption peak at 276 nm gradually increases, indicating that more CdSe precipitates are converted into CdSe QDs. That is, the concentration of CdSe QDs solutions increases with the extension of the HC cycle time. Meanwhile, with the extension of the HC cycle time, the absorption peaks become sharper and sharper, indicating that the particle size distribution range of CdSe QDs becomes narrower.

The fluorescence spectra of the prepared CdSe QDs solutions within different HC cycle time are tested and the results are shown in Fig. 2(e and f). The fluorescence emission spectra of the CdSe QDs (9.0 h) under different excitation wavelengths (202 nm–302 nm) are shown in Fig. 2(e). It can be seen from the figure that with the increase of excitation wavelength, the peak position of the emission spectrum is first blue-shifting and then red-shifting. When the CdSe QDs solution is excited at 202 nm–272 nm, the fluorescence peak shifts gradually from 367 nm to 359 nm. When the excitation wavelength is 272 nm–302 nm, the fluorescence peak wavelength of CdSe QDs increases from 359 nm to 366 nm with the increase of excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CdSe QDs solution changes obviously, that is, first increasing and then decreasing with the increase of excitation wavelength. This changing trend can be seen more clearly in Fig. 2(e–1). Specifically, when the excitation wavelength is < 272 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CdSe QDs solution gradually increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength and the strongest emission peak appears at 272 nm excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is > 272 nm, the fluorescence intensity gradually weakens with the increase of excitation wavelength. It shows that the obtained CdSe QDs solution exhibits an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. Fig. 2(f) shows the emission spectra of CdSe QDs solutions obtained within different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 9.0 h and 12 h) at 272 nm excitation wavelength. The emission peak range of prepared CdSe QDs solution is 300 nm–500 nm. The emission spectra show that the fluorescence intensity is located at 359 nm and gradually increases with the extension of HC cycle time. This may be due to the fact that the concentration of CdSe QDs solution increases and the particle size of CdSe QDs decreases with the extension of HC cycle time. In the figure, a large Stokes shift (87 nm) is also observed and the CdSe QDs solutions all emit blue light under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light. Meanwhile, the emitted light becomes brighter, which demonstrates that the particle size of CdSe QDs gradually becomes uniform and the concentration of CdSe QDs solution increases with the extension HC cycle time. In conclusion, appropriately extending the HC cycle time can reduce the particle size and improve the optical properties of CdSe QDs.

As one of the important factors affecting the HC effect, the inlet pressure can decide the fluid velocity and the number of cavitation bubbles [39], and then controls the particle sizes, morphology and performance of prepared CdSe QDs. Firstly, the CdSe QDs prepared under different inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar) are all tested by using XRD to analyze the crystal structure and chemical composition. The XRD images of these samples are shown in Fig. 3 (a). The corresponding standard card of sphalerite CdSe (JCPDS #19–0191) is given as the comparison of characteristic peaks. Being consistent with the standard card, the patterns of three CdSe QDs samples all show three diffraction peaks located at 2θ values of 25.35°, 42.01° and 49.70°, respectively, corresponding to the (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes. Therefore, it can be judged that the CdSe QDs with sphalerite crystal structure have been successfully prepared. It can also be observed from the figure that the XRD patterns of CdSe QDs (2.0 bar), CdSe QDs (3.0 bar) and CdSe QDs (4.0 bar) show broad diffraction peaks and the order of diffraction peak widths is CdSe QDs (3.0 bar) > CdSe QDs (4.0 bar) > CdSe QDs (2.0 bar) [27]. The broadening of the diffraction peaks may be caused by the decrease of the CdSe QDs particle sizes [28]. In conclusion, CdSe QDs with small average particle size can be obtained under an appropriate HC inlet pressure (3.0 bar).

Then, the morphology and size of the prepared CdSe QDs are tested by TEM. The corresponding TEM images are shown in Fig. 3(b–d) and the insets in the upper right corner of three figures show the particle size distribution of the prepared CdSe QDs. It can be observed that CdSe QDs are spherical and have good dispersion. Moreover, the average particle sizes of CdSe QDs are 3.20 nm (2.0 bar), 2.49 nm (3.0 bar) and 2.85 nm (4.0 bar) and the size distribution ranges of CdSe QDs are 1.73 nm–5.19 nm, 1.53 nm–3.52 nm and 2.30 nm–4.09 nm, respectively. Obviously, either too high or too low inlet pressure is not conducive to the preparation of CdSe QDs with small particle size and uniform size distribution. The possible reasons for the variation in particle size and size distribution range of CdSe QDs are as follows. When the inlet pressure is 2.0 bar, the speed of CdSe suspension flowing through the venturi tube is the slowest (19.76 m/s) and the corresponding C v is the largest (0.417). This indicates that the HC effect is the weakest, that is, the HC system releases the least amount of energy. Therefore, the CdSe QDs obtained under 2.0 bar inlet pressure have the largest average particle size and the widest size distribution range. At an inlet pressure of 4.0 bar, the flow rate of the CdSe suspension is the fastest (42.09 m/s) and the corresponding C v is the smallest (0.092). However, the excessively fast flow velocity can increase the colliding probability of cavitation bubbles with each other, so that large amounts of cavitation bubbles will fuse to form cavitation clouds. The formation of a cavitation cloud will reduce the number of cavitation bubbles, that is, the so-called super-cavitation phenomenon occurs. Therefore, when the inlet pressure of the venturi tube HC system is 4.0 bar, the average particle size of CdSe QDs is larger. When the inlet pressure is 3.0 bar, the flow rate of the CdSe suspension is relatively fast (29.25 m/s) and the corresponding C v is relatively small (0.190), representing that a strong HC effect occurs. The strong HC effect results in the formation and collapse of a large number of cavitation bubbles and the release of a large amount of energy. Therefore, the average particle size is small when the inlet pressure of the venturi tube HC system is increased to 3.0 bar. In conclusion, a strong HC effect can be obtained, when the inlet pressure is in the range of 3.0 bar–4.0 bar. And that, CdSe QDs with small particle size and uniform distribution can be obtained, when the inlet pressure is in appropriate range.

In order to study the optical properties of the prepared CdSe QDs solutions, the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of CdSe QDs (2.0 bar), CdSe QDs (3.0 bar) and CdSe QDs (4.0 bar) solutions are tested and test results are shown in Fig. 3(e and f). From Fig. 3(e), it can be seen that the UV–vis spectra of three CdSe QDs solutions all display an absorption peak at 276 nm in the range of 250 nm–300 nm. The order of the absorption peak intensities is CdSe QDs (3.0 bar) > CdSe QDs (4.0 bar) > CdSe QDs (2.0 bar). It can be considered that the HC effect is strongest when the inlet pressure is 3.0 bar, leading to the most effective fragmentation of CdSe precipitates. Thus, the CdSe QDs solution with the highest concentration is obtained. Fig. 3(f) shows the emission spectra of CdSe QDs (2.0 bar), CdSe QDs (3.0 bar) and CdSe QDs (4.0 bar) solutions under 226 nm wavelength light excitation. The fluorescence spectra show that the emission peaks are all located at 359 nm and the order of peak intensities is CdSe QDs (3.0 bar) > CdSe QDs (4.0 bar) > CdSe QDs (2.0 bar). This also manifests that the concentration of CdSe QDs (3.0 bar) solution is the highest. Under inlet pressures of 2.0 bar and 4.0 bar, the obtained CdSe QDs solutions have lower concentrations due to the weakened HC effect. Furthermore, the inset in Fig. 3(f) shows the photo of the luminescence of the CdSe QDs solutions. The CdSe QDs solutions all emit blue light under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light and the brightness order is consistent with the order of fluorescence intensities, that is, selecting an appropriate inlet pressure (3.0 bar) contributes to high optical properties of CdSe QDs solution. Moreover, the stability study of CdSe QDs solutions under different inlet pressures at different HC cycle times are shown in the Supporting Information (S1.1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of venturi tube geometry (half convergent angle β, half divergent angle α, and throat diameter) on CdSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The half convergent angle as an important geometrical parameter of the venturi tube can affect the HC strength inside the venturi tube. By optimizing the half convergent angle, a strong HC effect can be obtained, thereby obtained CdSe QDs have small particle sizes and good optical properties. Firstly, the XRD of CdSe QDs prepared in venturi tubes HC system with different half convergent angles (20°, 22° and 24°) are determined to analyze their crystal structure and chemical composition. The XRD images of these samples are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The corresponding standard card of sphalerite CdSe (JCPDS #19–0191) is also given as a comparison of characteristic peaks. It can be seen from the figure that the patterns of three CdSe QDs samples all show three diffraction peaks located at 2θ values of 25.35°, 42.01° and 49.70°, respectively, corresponding to (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes, which are consistent with the positions of the characteristic peak given by the standard card. Therefore, it can be judged that the CdSe QDs with sphalerite crystal structure have been successfully prepared. It can also be observed from the figure that the XRD patterns of the prepared CdSe QDs show broad diffraction peaks, and the order of diffraction peak widths is CdSe QDs (22°) > CdSe QDs (24°) > CdSe QDs (20°). This result shows that the particle size order of the CdSe QDs is CdSe QDs (22°) < CdSe QDs (24°) < CdSe QDs (20°). In conclusion, in the process of preparing CdSe QDs by using HC method, when the half convergent angle of the venturi tube is 22°, CdSe QDs with the smallest particle size can be obtained. Then, TEM is used to confirm the morphology and size of CdSe QDs. The corresponding TEM images are shown in Fig. 4(b–d) and the insets in the upper right corner of three figures are the corresponding particle size distribution maps. It can be observed that CdSe QDs are spherical and have good dispersion. Moreover, the average particle sizes of CdSe QDs (20°), CdSe QDs (22°) and CdSe QDs (24°) are 3.18 nm, 2.56 nm and 3.03 nm, respectively. The size distribution ranges of CdSe QDs (20°), CdSe QDs (22°) and CdSe QDs (24°) are 1.26 nm–5.34 nm, 2.31 nm–3.58 nm and 2.05 nm–5.01 nm, respectively. Obviously, too large or too small half convergent angle is not conducive to the preparation of CdSe QDs with small particle size and uniform size distribution.

Shi et al. reported that the venturi tube with a relatively large half convergent angle is conducive to the occurrence of HC phenomenon. This is because the velocity of fluid decreases more obviously when the fluid passes through a venturi tube with a relatively large half convergent angle, resulting in a relatively large pressure drop within the fluid [40]. The flow velocities of the CdSe suspension in the venturi tubes with β = 20°, β = 22° and β = 24° are 47.97 m/s, 29.25 m/s and 15.66 m/s, respectively, and the calculated C v are 0.089, 0.190 and 0.839, respectively. The pressure drops in the venturi tube with β = 22° is relatively large. So, when the CdSe suspension flows through the venturi tube with β = 22°, the internal pressure of the liquid can quickly fall below the saturated vapor pressure, which is conducive to the generation of the HC effect. However, an overlarge half convergent angle (β = 24°) will lead to collision and friction between liquid molecules when the liquid enters the venturi tube throat. It increases the flow resistance, which is not conducive to the formation and growth of cavitation bubbles, thus weakening the HC effect. Therefore, due to the collision and friction between liquid molecules and the formation of turbulence, the venturi tube with β = 24° has a relatively weak HC effect. To sum up, for these three venturi tubes, the HC system of the venturi tube with β = 22° produces the strongest HC effect and can effectively break CdSe precipitates, so CdSe QDs with small and uniform particle sizes can be obtained.

In order to study the optical properties of the prepared CdSe QDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CdSe QDs (20°), CdSe QDs (22°) and CdSe QDs (24°) solutions are tested. The results are shown in Fig. 4(e and f). As can be seen in Fig. 4(e), the UV–vis spectra of three CdSe QDs solutions all display an absorption peak at 276 nm in the range of 250 nm–300 nm, and the order of absorption peak intensities is CdSe QDs (22°) > CdSe QDs (24°) > CdSe QDs (20°). It can be considered that the CdSe QDs (22°) solution with the highest concentration is obtained due to the strongest HC effect. Furthermore, the absorption peak of CdSe QDs (22°) solution has the narrowest width, and it can be inferred that the particle size distribution range of CdSe QDs (22°) is the narrowest. The above analyses are consistent with the TEM results. Fig. 4(f) shows the emission spectra of CdSe QDs (20°), CdSe QDs (22°) and CdSe QDs (24°) solutions under 226 nm wavelength excitation. The emission peak ranges of the prepared CdSe QDs solutions are all 300 nm–500 nm. The fluorescence spectra show that the emission peaks are all located at 359 nm, and the order of peak intensities is CdSe QDs (22°) > CdSe QDs (24°) > CdSe QDs (20°). This shows that the concentration of CdSe QDs (22°) solution is the highest. For the venturi tube with β = 20° and β = 24°, due to the weak HC effect, the obtained CdSe QDs solutions have lower concentration. Furthermore, as can be seen from the inset, the CdSe QDs solutions all emit blue light under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, and the brightness order of the CdSe QDs solutions is CdSe QDs (22°) > CdSe QDs (24°) > CdSe QDs (20°).

The half divergent angle (α) of the venturi tube has a great influence on the lifetime of cavitation bubbles and the pressure recovery rate [41,42]. Therefore, the value of α will affect the strength of HC, and then control the size, morphology and properties of prepared CdSe QDs. Firstly, the XRD patterns of CdSe QDs prepared in venturi tube HC systems with different half divergent angles (4.0°, 6.0° and 8.0°) are obtained to analyze the crystal structure and chemical composition of the CdSe QDs. The XRD images of these samples are shown in Fig. 5 (a). The corresponding standard card of sphalerite CdSe (JCPDS #19–0191) is also given as a comparison of characteristic peaks. It can be seen from the figure that the patterns of three CdSe QDs samples all show three diffraction peaks located at 2θ values of 25.35°, 42.01° and 49.70°, corresponding to (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes, which are consistent with the positions of the characteristic peak given by the standard card. Therefore, it can be judged that the CdSe QDs with sphalerite crystal structure have been successfully prepared. It can also be observed from the figure that the XRD patterns of the prepared CdSe QDs show broad diffraction peaks, and the order of diffraction peak widths is CdSe QDs (6°) > CdSe QDs (8°) > CdSe QDs (4°). In conclusion, in the process of preparing CdSe QDs by using HC method, when the half diffraction angle of the venturi tube is 6.0°, CdSe QDs with the smallest particle size can be obtained.

Subsequently, TEM is used to confirm the morphology and size of CdSe QDs. Fig. 5(b–d) displays the TEM images of CdSe QDs (4°), CdSe QDs (6°) and CdSe QDs (8°), respectively. The insets in the upper right corner of three figures are the corresponding particle size distribution maps. It can be observed that the CdSe QDs are spherical and have good dispersion. The average particle sizes of CdSe QDs (4°), CdSe QDs (6°) and CdSe QDs (8°) are 3.14 nm, 2.49 nm and 2.90 nm, respectively. The size distribution ranges of CdSe QDs (4°), CdSe QDs (6°) and CdSe QDs (8°) are 2.10 nm–4.50 nm, 1.53 nm–3.52 nm and 1.83 nm–4.19 nm, respectively. For venturi tube with a small half divergence angle (4.0°), when the CdSe suspension flows through this venturi tube, it has a large flow velocity (52.6 m/s). Under the action of high-speed jet, the generated cavities are pushed out of the cavitation zone before they grow up. Through calculation, a small C v (0.067) is obtained, which means the occurrence of super-cavitation. On the contrary, for the venturi tube with α = 8.0°, the flow velocity is 23.70 m/s, and the C v is 0.328, which means a weak HC effect. Although there are a large number of cavities generated in the throat of venturi tube, when the CdSe suspension flows through the divergent section, the internal pressure of the liquid is immediately recovered, resulting in the sudden collapse of the cavities. On the other hand, the local eddy currents will be produced when CdSe suspension flows through the large divergent section, which results in significant energy loss [43]. By comparison, the smallest particle size CdSe QDs can be obtained in the venturi tube HC system with α = 6.0°. This is because an appropriate flow rate (29.25 m/s) can be obtained and a small C v (0.190) can be given when the CdSe suspension flows through the venturi tube with α = 6.0°. In this venturi tube, the pressure can be smoothly recovered and the cavities can grow into the real cavitation bubbles before collapsing, resulting in a strong HC effect [44]. In conclusion, when the half divergent angle of the venturi tube is appropriate, such as 6.0°, a strong HC effect can be generated, thereby preparing CdSe QDs with small and uniform sizes.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the CdSe QDs (4°), CdSe QDs (6°) and CdSe QDs (8°) solutions are tested. The results are shown in Fig. 5(e and f). As can be seen in Fig. 5(e), the UV–vis spectra of three CdSe QDs solutions all display an absorption peak at 276 nm in the range of 250 nm–300 nm and the order of absorption peak intensities is CdSe QDs (6.0°) > CdSe QDs (8.0°) > CdSe QDs (4.0°). It can be considered that the CdSe QDs (6°) solution with the highest concentration is obtained due to the strongest HC effect among these three venturi tube HC systems. As can be seen in Fig. 5(f), the emission spectra of CdSe QDs (4°), CdSe QDs (6°) and CdSe QDs (8°) solutions under 226 nm wavelength excitation are compared. The fluorescence spectra show that the emission peaks are all located at 359 nm and the order of peak intensities is CdSe QDs (6°) > CdSe QDs (8°) > CdSe QDs (4°). This also shows that the concentration of CdSe QDs (6°) solution is the highest. For the venturi tube HC systems with 4.0° and 8.0° half divergent angles, the obtained CdSe QDs solutions have lower concentrations due to the weak HC effect. Furthermore, as is shown in the inset, the CdSe QDs solutions all emit blue light under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, and the brightness order of the CdSe QDs solutions is CdSe QDs (6°) > CdSe QDs (8°) > CdSe QDs (4°).

The throat of the venturi tube is the restricted area of the HC device. As fluid passes through the throat of the venturi tube, it is accelerated and depressurized. Therefore, the sizes of venturi tubes throat diameters will affect the intensity of HC effect, and then affect the morphology, size and optical property of CdSe QDs. Firstly, the XRD tests were conducted on CdSe QDs prepared in venturi tubes HC system with different throat diameters (1.0 mm, 2.0 mm and 4.0 mm) to analyze their crystal structure and chemical composition. The XRD patterns of these CdSe QDs are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The standard card of sphalerite CdSe (JCPDS #19–0191) is also given as a characteristic peak comparison. From the figure, it can be seen that three CdSe QDs all show three diffraction peaks at 25.35°, 42.01° and 49.70°, corresponding to (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes, which are consistent with the characteristic peak positions given by the standard card. Therefore, it can be considered that CdSe QDs with sphalerite crystal structure are successfully prepared. It can also be observed from the figure that the XRD patterns of the prepared CdSe QDs show broad diffraction peaks and the order of diffraction peak widths is CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (4.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (1.0 mm). Since the broadening of the diffraction peak is caused by the decrease of particle sizes, the above result indicates that CdSe QDs with small particle sizes can be obtained when the throat diameter is 2.0 mm in the preparing process of CdSe QDs by using HC method.

Subsequently, TEM is used to confirm the morphology and size of CdSe QDs. Fig. 6(b–d) displays the TEM images of CdSe QDs (1.0 mm), CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) and CdSe QDs (4.0 mm). The insets in the upper right corner of three figures are the corresponding particle size distribution maps. It can be observed that the CdSe QDs are spherical and have good dispersion. The average particle sizes of CdSe QDs (1.0 mm), CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) and CdSe QDs (4.0 mm) are 3.27 nm, 2.49 nm and 2.94 nm, respectively. The size distribution ranges are 2.51 nm–4.33 nm, 1.53 nm–3.52 nm and 1.70 nm–4.16 nm, respectively. Obviously, too long or too short throat diameter of the venturi tube is not conducive to the preparation of CdSe QDs with small particle size and uniform size distribution. For the venturi tube with a small throat diameter (1.0 mm), the average flow velocity of liquid through the throat is relatively fast (57.60 m/s). By calculation, a very small C v (0.062) is given, which heralds the occurrence of super-cavitation phenomenon. Although a large number of cavities can be generated during this process, these cavities quickly leave the cavitation zone and are unlikely to grow into cavitation bubbles. Therefore, due to the occurrence of super-cavitation, the HC effect is weak in this system. When the throat diameter is 4.0 mm, the average flow velocity of liquid through the throat decreases to 16.24 m/s, resulting in a large C v (0.780). Although cavities can stay in the cavitation zone for a relatively long time and grow into cavitation bubbles, the slow flow velocity leads to the generation of fewer cavities. Therefore, the HC effect is also weak in this system. However, when the throat diameter is 2.0 mm, the average flow velocity is 29.25 m/s. The calculated C v is 0.190, which heralds the occurrence of strong HC phenomenon. The resulting pressure drop at this flow rate can lead to the generation of a large number of cavities and these cavities can grow into cavitation bubbles in a relatively sufficient time. The enormous energy released by the collapse of the cavitation bubbles effectively breaks the CdSe precipitates. In conclusion, when the throat diameter of the venturi tube is appropriate, such as 2.0 mm, the strong HC effect can be generated, leading to the preparation of CdSe QDs with small and uniform sizes.

In order to study the optical properties of the prepared CdSe QDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the CdSe QDs solutions obtained in the venturi tube HC systems with different throat diameters (1.0 mm, 2.0 mm and 4.0 mm) are tested. The results are shown in Fig. 6(e and f). As can be seen in Fig. 6(e), the UV–vis spectra of three CdSe QDs solutions all display an absorption peak at 276 nm in the range of 250 nm–300 nm and the order of absorption peak intensities is CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (4.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (1.0 mm). It can be considered that the CdSe QDs solution with the highest concentration is obtained due to the strongest HC effect when the throat diameter is 2.0 mm in these three venturi tube HC systems. Furthermore, the absorption peak of the CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) solution has the narrowest width, and it can be inferred that the particle size distribution range of the CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) is the narrowest for these three CdSe QDs particles. The above analyze is consistent with the TEM result. As can be seen in Fig. 6(f), the emission spectra of CdSe QDs (1.0 mm), CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) and CdSe QDs (4.0 mm) solutions under 226 nm wavelength excitation are compared. The emission peak ranges of the prepared CdSe QDs solutions are all 300 nm–500 nm. The fluorescence spectra show that the emission peaks are all located at 359 nm and the order of peak intensities is CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (4.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (1.0 mm). This also shows that the concentration of CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) solution is the highest. For the venturi tube HC systems with 1.0 mm and 4.0 mm throat diameters, the obtained CdSe QDs solutions have lower concentration due to the weak HC effect. Furthermore, as is shown in the inset, the CdSe QDs solutions all emit blue light under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, and the brightness order of the CdSe QDs solutions is CdSe QDs (2.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (4.0 mm) > CdSe QDs (1.0 mm). In addition, the stability study of CdSe QDs solutions in HC systems with different geometries of venturi tube are shown in the Supporting Information (S1.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism and process of preparing CdSe QDs by using HC method
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above analysis results, a possible mechanism of preparing water-soluble CdSe QDs by using the venturi tube HC method is proposed and the related figure is shown in Fig. 7 . In the HC reactor, when the CdSe suspension flows through the venturi tube, its speed increases suddenly due to the throttling effect of the venturi tube. According to the Bernoulli principle, a sudden speed increase will cause a rapid pressure drop. When the internal pressure of the fluid falls below the saturated vapor pressure, part of the liquid is evaporated and vaporized to form a large number of cavities. With the further decrease of the internal pressure of the fluid, the cavities grow gradually and form unstable cavitation bubbles. After that, these cavitation bubbles enter the pressure recovery zone with the flow of fluid. In the pressure recovery zone, the flow velocity decreases gradually and the internal pressure of the liquid increases. When the pressure recovers to a certain level, the cavitation bubbles are squeezed and then collapse, releasing a large amount of energy and producing high temperature (5000 K–10,000 K), high pressure (500 atm–1000 atm), strong shear force, shock wave and high-speed jet (400 km/h). These physical effects work together on CdSe precipitates.

Specifically, under the action of high pressure, large CdSe precipitates are squeezed and broken into small CdSe particles. Under the action of multiple shock waves and jets, CdSe particles are broken into smaller particles. Shearing force can make the broken CdSe particles to collide with each other and rub repeatedly, which further makes the CdSe particles smaller and the surface smoother. After a long period of HC cycle, CdSe particles will eventually be broken into CdSe QDs and the CdSe QDs will have a smooth surface and spherical shape due to collision and friction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CdSe QDs (12 h) in Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen in Fig. 2(f), the CdSe QDs prepared in this study possess a large Stokes shift (87 nm), which indicates that CdSe QDs should have several advantageous properties for detection applications, such as low background interference, low sample photodamage, strong penetrability and high sensitivity [45]. Therefore, the prepared CdSe QDs can serve as probes for detecting metal ions. The fluorescence emission spectra of CdSe QDs (12 h) in the presence of various metal ions at 10 μmol/L concentration are showed in Fig. 8 (a) and the relative fluorescence intensities of CdSe QDs (12 h) solutions in the presence and absence of various metal ions are displayed in Fig. 8(b). By comparison, among these metal ions, Fe3+ ions show the highest fluorescence quenching effect towards CdSe QDs (12 h), giving a quenching effect of 80 %. The fluorescence quenching effects of Ag+, Mg2+, Sn2+ and Cu2+ are 45 %, 29 %, 25 % and 11 %, respectively. While Ba2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+ and Na + hardly cause fluorescence quenching towards CdSe QDs (12 h).

Furthermore, the detection sensitivity of CdSe QDs to Fe3+ is studied by varying the concentration of Fe3+ and the corresponding quenching mechanism is proposed. Different concentrations of Fe3+ are added to the CdSe QDs (12 h) solutions and the fluorescence emission intensities are measured. Fig. 8(c) shows that the fluorescence emission intensity of the CdSe QDs (12 h) solutions under the excitation of 272 nm wavelength light gradually decreases with the Fe3+ concentration increases from 0 to 20 μmol/L. Additionally, high concentrations of Fe3+ (>1.0 μmol/L) lead to the red shift of the fluorescence peak. This may be due to the cation exchange reaction, leading to the formation of Fe2Se3 nanoparticles on the surface of CdSe QDs (12 h) [46].

Fluorescence quenching is divided into dynamic quenching and static quenching. Dynamic quenching refers to the interaction between a quencher and an excited state molecule of the fluorophore, which leads to the reduction of fluorescence intensity [47]. Static quenching refers to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex through the coordination of a quencher and a ground-state molecule of the fluorophore, which leads to the reduction of fluorescence intensity [48]. The fluorescence lifetimes of CdSe QDs (12 h) in the absence and presence of Fe3+ are measured and the results are shown in Fig. 8(d). The change of the fluorescence lifetime is not obvious when Fe3+ is added in CdSe QDs (12 h) solution, which is consistent with the characteristic of static quenching [49]. Fig. 8(e) shows the Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence quenching of CdSe QDs (12 h) in the presence of Fe3+ at different test temperatures as well as the value of τ0/τ with the change of Fe3+concentration in the range of 0–20 μmol/L. The correlation coefficient (R2) is above 0.99, suggesting a good linear relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration [49]. It can be seen from the figure that the slope value of the fitting line of F0/F value measured under lower experimental temperature (25 °C) is greater than corresponding one of the fitting lines obtained under the higher experimental temperature (45 °C). This indicates that fluorescence quenching of CdSe QDs (12 h) in the presence of Fe3+ belongs to a static quenching process [50–53]. In addition, comparing the fluorescence lifetime (τ0) of CdSe QDs solution measured without Fe3+ with that of CdSe QDs solution with different Fe3+ concentrations, it is found that the ratio (τ0/τ) is almost a fixed value (1.0) and the results are shown in Table 2 . This again suggests that fluorescence quenching of CdSe QDs solution caused by Fe3+ belongs to a static quenching process [54].

Moreover, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+ is also explored by comparing the size of relative reduction potential. The selective fluorescence quenching towards CdSe QDs (12 h) by Fe3+ can be explained based on the relative reduction potentials of metal ions and CdSe QDs. Charge transfer is thermodynamically favorable only when the relative reduction potentials of the metal ions are more positive than that of CdSe QDs (−0.2 V vs. NHE). The relative reduction potential of Fe3+ is +0.771 V, while those of Ba2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, Sn2+ and Cu2+ are −2.91 V, −2.87 V, −0.76 V, −0.26 V, −2.71 V, −2.37 V, −0.14 V and +0.34 V, respectively. Therefore, based on the thermodynamic, Fe3+ has a greater driving force of the charge transfer [55]. It is worth noting that the relative reduction potential of Ag+ is +0.799 V, which is slightly higher than that of Fe3+. However, Fe3+ is more easily binds to the hydroxyl group on the surface of CdSe QDs due to its high charge, resulting in fluorescence quenching.

According to formula (4), the limit of detection (LOD) is 4.98 μmol/L. This is superior to most of the reported fluorescent probes (as shown in Table 3 ).
(4)
L O D =
K S
m
Where S is the standard deviation of the data obtained from multiple consecutive measurements of the CdSe QDs (12 h) solution. In this study, the fluorescence intensities of CdSe QDs solution measured continuously for 10 times are used as the basis for calculating the standard deviation. K is a constant related to the confidence level and K = 3 in this study. m is the slope of the fitting curve.

Finally, the feasibility and reliability of CdSe QDs (12 h) as a fluorescent probe are evaluated by detecting different concentrations of Fe3+. The experimental results are shown in Table 4 . It can be seen from the table that the measured values are very close to the actual concentration of Fe3+ in solution. The fluorescence recovery efficiency is above 96 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is below 3 %. In addition, the results obtained by fluorescence probe method in this experiment are basically consistent with those measured by ICP-MS method. This indicates that this method can be used to determine the concentration of Fe3+ in real samples, which has a high accuracy.

In summary, using Cd(NO3)2·4H2O and selenium powder as raw materials, blue-emitting CdSe QDs are successfully prepared by using HC technology. In this work, it is found that the prepared CdSe QDs have small particle size (1.83 nm), large Stokes shift (87 nm) and long fluorescence lifetime (1255 ns) when the HC cycle time is 12 h, the inlet pressure is 3.0 bar, the throat diameter of the venturi tube is 2.0 mm, the half divergence angle is 6.0° and the half convergence angle is 22°. The possible generation mechanism of CdSe QDs by using HC method was proposed. That is, the high temperature, high pressure, high-speed jet, shock wave and shear force generated in the HC system can convert large CdSe particles into CdSe QDs. This work establishes the foundation for large-scale preparation of quantum dots. At the same time, it is the first time to elaborate the HC preparation method of CdSe QDs. In addition, the experimental results show that CdSe QDs have high sensitivity and selectivity for the analysis and detection of Fe3+ in multiple metal ions solution. CdSe QDs (12 h) was used as the fluorescence probe for Fe3+ analysis and the limit of detection was 4.98 μmol/L. This means that CdSe QDs have a potential application prospect as a fluorescent probe in Fe3+ detection. However, the 12 h HC treatment time of CdSe precipitate is still relatively long. In the future, the preparation method of CdSe QDs with small particle size, narrow size distribution range and strong luminescence capacity will be further improved through designing reasonable HC equipment and optimizing the geometrical parameters of HC device.

Jince Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xiaochen Qi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Taiyu Jin: Formal analysis. Di Fang: Validation, Supervision. Yonghao Li: Validation, Investigation. Ziyue Zhou: Validation, Supervision. Haishuo Wu: Supervision, Investigation. Jun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Dawei Fang: Visualization, Funding acquisition.
